items.question_text: Complete: Under 7-7-1, apply TRSA procedures within the designated TRSA in addition to the basic services described in Chapter 7, Section _____.
items.explanation: Per 7-7-1, TRSA procedures are applied in addition to (not instead of) the basic services in Section 6 (Basic Radar Service to VFR Aircraft). TRSA is an overlay, not a replacement.

items.question_text: Under 7-7-3, what methods may be used to separate participating VFR aircraft from IFR aircraft in a TRSA?
items.explanation: Per 7-7-3, separation may be by any one of: visual separation (per 7-2-1), radar separation (per Chapter 5), nonradar separation (per Chapter 6), or pilot-applied visual separation (per 7-2-1). This gives controllers flexibility in TRSA separation methods.

choices.text: Visual separation, radar separation, nonradar separation, or pilot-applied visual separation.
Only radar separation, because TRSA operations require radar coverage.
Only visual separation, because TRSA aircraft are all VFR.
Only nonradar separation, because TRSA is a nonradar procedure.

items.question_text: Under 7-7-4, must helicopters and powered-lift aircraft be separated from each other in a TRSA?
items.explanation: Per 7-7-4, helicopters and powered-lift aircraft need not be separated from other helicopters or powered-lift aircraft. However, traffic information must be exchanged as required by 2-1-21. This is a separation exemption, not a traffic information exemption.

choices.text: No, they need not be separated, but traffic information must be exchanged.
Yes, they must be separated by the same standards as fixed-wing aircraft.
No, and no traffic information is required because they have inherent maneuvering capability.
Only visual separation is required between helicopters and powered-lift aircraft.

items.question_text: Complete: Under 7-7-5a, altitude information contained in a clearance, instruction, or advisory to VFR aircraft must meet MVA, MSA, or minimum _____ altitude criteria.
items.explanation: Per 7-7-5a, any altitude assignment to VFR aircraft in a TRSA must meet at least one of: MVA (Minimum Vectoring Altitude), MSA (Minimum Safe Altitude), or minimum IFR altitude criteria. This ensures terrain and obstruction clearance even though the aircraft is VFR.

choices.text: IFR

items.question_text: Under 7-7-6, who specifies the approach interval in TRSA operations?
items.explanation: Per 7-7-6, the tower must specify the approach interval. This is a tower function — the tower determines how much spacing is needed between successive arrivals, and approach control sequences aircraft to meet that interval.

choices.text: The approach control facility.
The tower.
The pilot of the leading aircraft in the sequence.
The ARTCC sector controlling the arrival airspace.

items.situation: VFR aircraft are being held inside the TRSA due to arrival saturation at the primary airport. A pilot asks when to expect further clearance into the airport.
items.para_context: Per 7-7-2, when VFR aircraft are held either inside or outside the TRSA, the controller must inform the pilot when to expect further clearance (EFC).
items.question_text: What information must the controller provide to the holding VFR pilot?

choices.text: When to expect further clearance.
The current ATIS code and landing runway.
Only that the pilot should continue holding and stand by.
The delay is indefinite; no time estimate is required for VFR holding.

items.explanation: Per 7-7-2, the controller must inform VFR aircraft held inside or outside the TRSA when to expect further clearance. This is an EFC obligation similar to IFR holding.

items.instruction: The controller provided TRSA departure information. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: CONTACT DEPARTURE CONTROL ON ONE TWO SIX POINT EIGHT FIVE AFTER PASSING TWO THOUSAND FIVE HUNDRED.

choices.text: After two thousand five hundred, contact departure on one two six point eight five.
Contacting approach on one two six point eight five now.
Roger, maintain VFR at two thousand five hundred until further advised.
Wilco, switching to ground control.

items.explanation: Per 7-7-7a, at controlled airports within the TRSA, departing VFR aircraft proposing to operate within the TRSA are informed when to contact departure control and the frequency to use.

items.question_text: Under 7-7-5b, what happens when an altitude assigned to a VFR aircraft in a TRSA is contrary to 14 CFR 91.159?

choices.text: The pilot must advise ATC and ATC may assign an appropriate alternate altitude.
The pilot must comply regardless, because ATC altitude assignments override 14 CFR.
The assignment is automatically canceled and the pilot may choose any VFR altitude.
The pilot must file a pilot deviation report within 10 days.

items.explanation: Per 7-7-5b, if the assigned altitude is contrary to 14 CFR 91.159 (VFR cruising altitudes), the pilot must advise ATC, and ATC may then assign an appropriate alternate altitude.
